text:
hey folks one possible issue people have with 
writing web applications in Python is they

still have to learn at least some JavaScript or 
a JavaScript Centric framework to do something

useful but here and there we're seeing Solutions 
emerge that let you write full websites including

Rich front-end interactivity with nothing but a 
python backend today I'm going to look at one of

these projects called reflex reflex was originally 
called pine cone with a y when it was first rolled

out to the public in December of 2022 as of July 
2023 it's available as a public beta it's still

not recommended for Enterprise production work but 
it is useful enough to try out and build a top of

provisionally the basic idea with reflex is that 
you create web apps using components basically

python objects and these objects describe the 
structure of the page the look of the elements

and their behaviors such as what happens when 
you click a button reflex comes with a whole

gallery of built-in object types and the best 
part is how they reflect many common use cases

for instance everyone I know of struggles to take 
an element and Center it on a web page with reflex

you use the reflex. center element you set the 
height to 100% And you go about your business

it is literally that simple let me zoom in on a 
few examples a little more closely in this demo

we're creating a form with a counter and three 
buttons increment decrement and randomize this

block of nested objects here describes the layout 
of the the page and the dot button elements each

have onclick functions assigned to them the 
state for the page is held in a state object

which is a subass of a type reflex provides for 
managing State on a web page and you can also see

how inline CSS declarations can be supported 
easily if you want to include scripts or HTML

manually you can do that by other components 
too for instance if I wanted to add an existing

third-party JavaScript component like a rich 
text editor I could put that in by hand another

convenient thing reflex gives us is live reloading 
if I make a change to my application you can see

here that the app is restarted automatically 
and the changes are reflected almost instantly

this shortens the development cycle a good deal 
especially for things like constantly tweaking a

UI if you want to add your own custom components 
reflex provides a way to write them yourself or

you can take existing components written in react 
and deploy those that does mean you may have to

do some heavy lifting with react to achieve more 
advanced effects but the broad range of existing

react components out there means there's a good 
chance what you need already exists the best way

to get started with reflex in my opinion is to 
take a look at the gallery of examples on the

reflex site pull a couple of them that reflect use 
cases you have and see how they work for instance

if you wanted to create a database backed app and 
you want to see how reflexes built-in om supports

a given use case you can filter by database and 
choose something suitable now with any kind of web

application at some point you're going to need 
to Grapple directly with the nitty-gritty you

can't entirely put off firsthand experience with 
HTML and CSS and JavaScript and their Associated

Technologies but projects like reflex are meant 
to minimize the amount of tedium and context

switching associated with building such things 
they aren't meant to completely replace learning

about HTML or JavaScript but they're meant 
to make it easier for python users to build

things that use those Technologies so again 
while reflex is still an alpha product it's

worth experimenting with right now to see if 
it can make your python powered Web projects

easier to build and deploy so that's it for 
this episode if you liked it leave a comment

below and don't forget to follow Dev with SAR and 
infoworld on Facebook YouTube and infow
world.com
